summary: Jeff Atwood recounts a formative experience meeting an MIT-trained engineer who admitted he hadn't used anything from his coursework in his actual job. This initially shocking revelation led Atwood to understand that education's true value lies not in specific knowledge but in developing the ability to learn. He uses Wikipedia as an example of how modern tools can teach research and critical thinking skills far better than passive textbook reading. The post concludes that this meta-skill of learning how to learn is especially critical for software developers, whose field reinvents itself every decade.
key_points: The value of formal education isn't the specific material learned but developing the capacity to learn independently
Wikipedia represents an opportunity to teach students how knowledge is produced and verified rather than just consumed
Participatory, hands-on learning outperforms passive textbook reading
Software development requires continuous learning as the field reinvents itself roughly every decade
Understanding 'why' something works has lasting value while 'how' becomes obsolete within about five years
money_quotes: I can't think of a single thing from my MIT classes I've used on the job.
The value of education isn't in the specific material you learn – it's in learning how to learn.
Why read textbooks when you can help write one?
There's no substitute for learning on the battlefield.
Programming is, almost by definition, continuously learning: your entire career will be one long, unbroken string of learning one new bit of technology after another.
Every ten years the software development field reinvents itself, and it's our job to keep up.
If you don't like learning new things, you will despise software engineering. It's all we do.
In our field, how only lasts about five years, but why is forever.
key_insight: Software engineering demands mastering the skill of learning itself because specific technical knowledge becomes obsolete every few years, but the ability to acquire new knowledge remains valuable forever.
